loops.title: Code path explanation
loops.summary: Answer one codebase question with file evidence and no repo edits unless the user asks for a change.
bestFor: Understanding one flow, module, or behavior before changing it.
Answering a repo question with cited files and commands.
avoidWhen: The user already gave a verifier and wants edits.
A failing check needs repair.
The task should run on a schedule.
loops.defaultObjective: Explain the selected code path with evidence from the repository.
loops.defaultCheck: Cite the files inspected and run the narrowest read-only command if one is available.
stopConditions: Stop when the answer cites the relevant files and names any uncertainty.
Stop before editing files unless the user explicitly asks for changes.
questions: Which flow, command, route, or component should be explained?
loops.body: DISCOVER the relevant files, TRACE the runtime path, ANSWER with file evidence, and STOP without edits unless the user explicitly requests implementation.
requiredSignals: answer is useful without changing files
goodExamples: explain how checkout auth flow works without editing code
show me where package publish validation happens
what files control Loop It recommendation scoring
badExamples: fix failing CI typecheck job
publish npm package after final release checks
run production smoke every morning and report failures
exampleChecks: Cite the files inspected
Run a read-only command only when it clarifies the answer
Call out unknowns instead of guessing

commonMisroutes.query: fix failing CI
commonMisroutes.reason: A failing check needs a goal-based repair loop, not an explanation turn.

reliability.summary: Reliable for understanding work because it avoids edit loops and requires file evidence.
bestWhen: The question names a flow, command, module, or behavior.
The user wants understanding before implementation.
failsWhen: The user expects code changes.
The relevant behavior cannot be found locally.
reliability.requiredCheck: Cite the files inspected and run the narrowest read-only command if one is available.

userGuide.plainLanguage: Use this when you want the agent to explain code, not change it.
userGuide.useWhen: Pick this for one focused codebase question that should end with an evidence-backed answer.
userGuide.starterRequest: Explain how checkout auth flow works without editing code.
userGuide.firstStep: Find the files that own the flow and trace the path from entrypoint to outcome.
userGuide.proofTip: The answer should cite inspected files and separate facts from inferences.
userGuide.notFor: Not for repair, release, or scheduled automation work.

loops.title: Small edit verification
loops.summary: Make one scoped edit, run the narrow proof, and stop after reporting the changed files.
bestFor: A small known change with a nearby check.
Adding focused test or copy coverage without a multi-pass repair loop.
avoidWhen: The failing command is unknown.
The work requires multiple discovery passes.
The task should keep running after the turn.
loops.defaultObjective: Make the smallest scoped change and verify it with the narrowest relevant check.
loops.defaultCheck: Run the narrowest relevant test, lint, build, or manual smoke for the edited area.
stopConditions: Stop after one credible edit and one verification pass.
Stop if the check needs broader product or architecture decisions.
questions: What is the exact small change and what check should prove it?
loops.body: PLAN one small edit, APPLY it, VERIFY with the closest check, REPORT changed files and residual risk, then STOP.
goodExamples: make one small copy edit and verify lint still passes
add regression tests for an uncovered checkout edge case
fix one typo in setup docs and run markdown check
badExamples: fix failing CI typecheck job
inspect this repo and run the right loop
watch my PR every 10 minutes and address new review comments
exampleChecks: Run the edited package or file check
Show the changed files
State remaining risk if the check is manual

commonMisroutes.query: inspect this repo
commonMisroutes.reason: Broad repo requests need intake before a small edit is chosen.

reliability.summary: Reliable for low-risk work because it caps itself to one focused turn and one proof.
bestWhen: The expected edit is clear.
The check is nearby and cheap.
failsWhen: The issue requires diagnosis across multiple systems.
The verifier is missing or expensive.
reliability.requiredCheck: Run the narrowest relevant test, lint, build, or manual smoke for the edited area.

userGuide.plainLanguage: Use this for a small change that should finish in one pass.
userGuide.useWhen: Pick this when you already know the target and proof.
userGuide.starterRequest: Make one small copy edit and verify lint still passes.
userGuide.firstStep: Name the exact edit, then make only that edit.
userGuide.proofTip: Run the nearest check and report changed files.
userGuide.notFor: Not for multi-iteration repairs or background monitoring.

loops.title: Diff review pass
loops.summary: Review the current diff for regressions, missing tests, and release blockers without taking ownership of the whole feature.
bestFor: A human or agent already changed files and needs review.
Finding risks before commit, PR, or release.
avoidWhen: There is no diff to review.
The user asks to implement the feature from scratch.
New review comments should be polled over time.
loops.defaultObjective: Review the current diff and report blocking issues first.
loops.defaultCheck: Inspect git diff and run the narrowest relevant check if it is cheap and available.
stopConditions: Stop after findings, open questions, and verification notes are reported.
Stop before editing unless the user asks to fix findings.
questions: Should the review focus on bugs, security, UX, tests, or release risk?
loops.body: DISCOVER the diff, REVIEW for behavior and test risks, optionally VERIFY with a cheap check, REPORT findings first, then STOP.
goodExamples: review this diff for risky changes before merge
check my staged changes for missing tests
pre-merge review the Loop It runner changes
badExamples: address new review comments every 15 minutes
fix failing CI typecheck job
explain how checkout auth flow works without editing code
exampleChecks: Run git diff or git status
Run a narrow check only when useful
Report findings ordered by severity

commonMisroutes.query: address blocking pull request review comments
commonMisroutes.reason: A stream of new review comments is proactive workflow work, not a one-shot diff review.

reliability.summary: Reliable when there is a concrete diff because it uses code-review output rules.
bestWhen: There are local changed files.
The desired output is review findings, not implementation.
failsWhen: There is no diff.
The user needs ongoing PR monitoring.
reliability.requiredCheck: Inspect git diff and run the narrowest relevant check if it is cheap and available.

userGuide.plainLanguage: Use this when you want a review of changes already made.
userGuide.useWhen: Pick this before merging, releasing, or asking another reviewer.
userGuide.starterRequest: Review this diff for risky changes before merge.
userGuide.firstStep: Inspect git status and the relevant diff.
userGuide.proofTip: List blocker findings first with file references.
userGuide.notFor: Not for implementing a new feature or polling PR comments.

loops.title: Error explanation debug
loops.summary: Interpret one error, stack trace, or log and choose the smallest next debugging action.
bestFor: One visible error where the next action is unclear.
Explaining likely cause before choosing repair work.
avoidWhen: The failing command and fix loop are already known.
The user needs repeated verifier passes.
The error comes from production and needs incident response.
loops.defaultObjective: Explain the error and identify the smallest safe next debugging or repair step.
loops.defaultCheck: Reproduce the error or inspect the command/log that produced it.
stopConditions: Stop when the likely cause, evidence, and next action are clear.
Stop if more logs, secrets, or production access are required.
questions: What exact command, log, or stack trace produced the error?
loops.body: READ the error, MAP it to code or configuration, EXPLAIN the likely cause, suggest or make the smallest safe next step, and STOP.
goodExamples: explain this TypeError stack trace and identify the likely fix
debug this npm install error from the pasted log
why does this route crash with undefined property
badExamples: fix failing CI typecheck job
sanitize unsafe user input before saving it
check CI every 5 minutes until the branch is green
exampleChecks: Quote only the relevant error line
Trace the error to likely source files
Name the next command to prove the theory

commonMisroutes.query: failing CI typecheck job
commonMisroutes.reason: A named failing check should go straight to the repair loop.

reliability.summary: Reliable for narrowing one visible failure before a full repair loop starts.
bestWhen: The error text is available.
The user wants diagnosis before edits.
failsWhen: The error requires private services or production-only context.
The failure needs a multi-pass verifier repair.
reliability.requiredCheck: Reproduce the error or inspect the command/log that produced it.

userGuide.plainLanguage: Use this when an error is confusing and you need the next move.
userGuide.useWhen: Pick this for one pasted error, stack trace, or log.
userGuide.starterRequest: Explain this TypeError stack trace and identify the likely fix.
userGuide.firstStep: Read the exact error and trace it to the nearest code path.
userGuide.proofTip: The answer should name the evidence and the next verifier command.
userGuide.notFor: Not for broad repairs once the failing command is already known.

loops.title: UI copy clarity pass
loops.summary: Improve a focused UI copy or clarity problem with a quick visual or manual verification pass.
bestFor: One screen or flow has confusing labels, helper text, or CTA wording.
A small UX copy pass should not become a redesign.
avoidWhen: The whole page needs redesign.
The issue is a failing UI test.
The work needs product research or analytics.
loops.defaultObjective: Make the focused UI copy easier to understand without expanding the flow.
loops.defaultCheck: Manually verify the affected screen at desktop and mobile widths, or run the relevant UI smoke test.
stopConditions: Stop when the primary action and next step are clear.
Stop if further changes are subjective without user feedback.
questions: Which screen and primary action should be clearer?
loops.body: INSPECT the screen, EDIT only the confusing copy or labels, VERIFY the action is clear on desktop and mobile, then STOP.
goodExamples: tighten confusing button copy on the onboarding screen
make the install CTA label clearer
rename the loop picker helper text so users know what to do
badExamples: redesign the whole page from scratch
fix failing CI typecheck job
run production smoke every morning and report failures
exampleChecks: Verify desktop and mobile text fit
Confirm the primary action is visible
Run a UI smoke test if one exists

commonMisroutes.query: whole page redesign
commonMisroutes.reason: A broad product change needs a concrete goal and verifier before copy polish.

reliability.summary: Reliable for tiny clarity fixes because it resists turning copy work into a redesign.
bestWhen: The confusing UI element is named.
The desired user action is known.
failsWhen: The product goal is undecided.
The requested change is broad visual redesign.
reliability.requiredCheck: Manually verify the affected screen at desktop and mobile widths, or run the relevant UI smoke test.

userGuide.plainLanguage: Use this for one confusing piece of interface copy.
userGuide.useWhen: Pick this when labels or helper text make the next action unclear.
userGuide.starterRequest: Tighten confusing button copy on the onboarding screen.
userGuide.firstStep: Find the UI element and identify the user action it should support.
userGuide.proofTip: Verify the edited text fits and the next action is clear.
userGuide.notFor: Not for full-page redesigns or multi-screen UX work.

loops.title: Codebase intake to running loop
loops.summary: Inspect a repository request, choose the highest-confidence loop, and run one bounded verifier-gated loop.
bestFor: Broad repo requests where the concrete loop is not obvious yet.
Turning a vague instruction into one runnable loop with a verifier.
avoidWhen: The user already selected a loop.
The failing command is known and should be run immediately.
The task is only a code explanation.
loops.defaultObjective: Inspect this repo, recommend the highest-confidence loop, and run it with proof.
loops.defaultCheck: Run the inferred verifier or ask for one if no safe project-specific check can be found.
stopConditions: Stop when one concrete loop has run to proof or a clear blocker.
Stop if no safe verifier can be inferred.
Stop when the next action needs user approval.
questions: What outcome should be true when the agent is done?
Which command proves the result if one exists?
loops.body: DISCOVER repo signals, SELECT the strongest loop, PLAN the verifier, EXECUTE one bounded run, VERIFY, and REPORT proof or blocker.
requiredSignals: needs inferred verifier or targeted question
goodExamples: inspect this repo and run the right loop
find the next actionable issue in this codebase
what should Loop It tackle next
improve this repo with the highest-confidence loop
badExamples: fix failing CI with logs
publish npm package after final release checks
sanitize auth bypass
exampleChecks: Infer and run npm run check, npm test, lint, typecheck, or build when available
Record the selected loop id and verifier in .loop-it/progress.json
Stop with one targeted verifier question when no safe check can be inferred

commonMisroutes.query: explain how checkout auth flow works without editing code
commonMisroutes.reason: Read-only explanation should stay turn-based.

reliability.summary: Reliable for starting work because it forces one selected loop and one verifier before execution.
bestWhen: The request is broad but repo-local.
The repo has package scripts or obvious checks.
failsWhen: The repo has no safe verifier.
The task depends on external systems or product decisions.
reliability.requiredCheck: Run the inferred verifier or ask for one if no safe project-specific check can be found.

userGuide.plainLanguage: Use this when you want Loop It to inspect the repo and pick the safest loop.
userGuide.useWhen: Pick this when the user asks what to tackle or says to improve the repo broadly.
userGuide.starterRequest: Inspect this repo and run the right loop.
userGuide.firstStep: Inspect package scripts, current progress, and obvious failure signals.
userGuide.proofTip: The run should report the selected loop, verifier, changed files, and blocker if any.
userGuide.notFor: Not for a known failing command or a read-only explanation.

loops.title: Failing CI repair
loops.summary: Repair a failing build, lint, type-check, or test job with the smallest verified change.
bestFor: One failing command or CI job with output.
Smallest safe change to make the check green.
avoidWhen: No failing command exists.
The work is a broad feature request.
The task is only code explanation.
loops.defaultObjective: Fix the failing check with the smallest safe change.
loops.defaultCheck: Run the failing command locally or the closest available equivalent.
stopConditions: Stop when the failing check exits 0.
Stop if the same failure repeats twice without new evidence.
Stop if the check requires unavailable services or approvals.
questions: What exact command or CI job is failing?
Can you provide the failure output?
loops.body: DISCOVER the failure, PLAN the smallest credible repair, EXECUTE the patch, VERIFY the failing command, ITERATE until green or blocked.
requiredSignals: specific failing command or CI job
goodExamples: fix failing CI typecheck job
fix failing checkout test
repair npm run check
make lint pass after dependency update
badExamples: feature quality is uncertain
fresh install fails for a new user
security risk with no CI failure
exampleChecks: Run the failing command locally
Rerun the CI-equivalent check
Broaden to npm run check after the narrow check passes

commonMisroutes.query: fix bug where checkout redirects to the wrong page
commonMisroutes.reason: A behavior bug with known repro should use ticket-to-verified-fix.

reliability.summary: Proven local pattern for commands that can be rerun until green.
bestWhen: There is a named failing command.
The failure reproduces locally or has logs.
failsWhen: The check depends on secrets or production services.
The failure is flaky and needs repeated-pass proof.
reliability.requiredCheck: Run the failing command locally or the closest available equivalent.

userGuide.plainLanguage: Use this when a check is red and the job is to make it green.
userGuide.useWhen: Pick this for CI, lint, build, type-check, or test failures.
userGuide.starterRequest: Fix failing CI typecheck job.
userGuide.firstStep: Run the failing command or closest local equivalent.
userGuide.proofTip: The final proof is the command exiting 0.
userGuide.notFor: Not for broad product bugs or scheduled monitoring.

loops.title: Ticket to verified fix
loops.summary: Turn a bug report or small defect into the smallest patch with proof.
bestFor: A concrete wrong behavior and expected behavior.
A small fix that needs a reproduction or regression check.
avoidWhen: The request is only a red CI command.
The expected behavior is unknown.
The task is a pure refactor.
loops.defaultObjective: Fix the reported behavior with the smallest credible patch.
loops.defaultCheck: Run the original reproduction and the narrowest relevant regression check.
stopConditions: Stop when the reproduction passes.
Stop if expected behavior is unclear.
Stop if the fix requires approval-sensitive work.
questions: What is the observed behavior?
What should happen instead?
How can we reproduce it?
loops.body: DISCOVER the repro, PLAN the smallest fix, EXECUTE the patch, VERIFY the repro and nearby regression check, then REPORT proof.
goodExamples: fix bug where checkout redirects to the wrong page
fix a broken checkout redirect
repair a customer-reported regression
make the repro pass with a focused patch
badExamples: red CI job with logs
broad refactor with no failing behavior
undefined product decision
exampleChecks: Run the original reproduction
Run the narrowest regression test
Run the related feature smoke

commonMisroutes.query: fix failing CI typecheck job
commonMisroutes.reason: A named failing check should use failing-ci-repair.

reliability.summary: Reliable when a bug has an observed result, expected result, and repro path.
bestWhen: The defect is specific.
There is a reproduction or narrow test.
failsWhen: The product decision is unresolved.
The issue is a general quality complaint.
reliability.requiredCheck: Run the original reproduction and the narrowest relevant regression check.

userGuide.plainLanguage: Use this for a real bug with expected behavior.
userGuide.useWhen: Pick this when something specific is wrong and can be reproduced.
userGuide.starterRequest: Fix bug where checkout redirects to the wrong page.
userGuide.firstStep: Reproduce the bug or identify the narrowest equivalent check.
userGuide.proofTip: The final proof should show the repro or regression check passing.
userGuide.notFor: Not for vague improvements or pure CI repairs.

loops.title: Security hardening
loops.summary: Reduce a concrete security risk with scoped evidence and approval gates.
bestFor: A specific security finding or trust-boundary risk.
Input validation, auth, secret exposure, or permission hardening with tests.
avoidWhen: The ask is a broad audit with no target.
The fix requires secret rotation or production changes without approval.
The task is just dependency monitoring.
loops.defaultObjective: Reduce the named security risk with the smallest scoped change.
loops.defaultCheck: Run the relevant security, auth, validation, or regression check.
stopConditions: Stop when the risk is mitigated and the regression check passes.
Stop if remediation requires credential changes or production access.
Stop if the finding cannot be reproduced or scoped.
questions: What is the specific risk or finding?
Which trust boundary is affected?
What check proves the risk is reduced?
loops.body: DISCOVER the trust boundary, PLAN the mitigation, EXECUTE the smallest safe change, VERIFY with security or regression proof, and REPORT residual risk.
goodExamples: sanitize unsafe user input before saving it
security review finding for auth bypass
fix authorization bypass
remove exposed secret from config path
badExamples: broad audit with no target
secret rotation without approval
generic code cleanup
exampleChecks: Run relevant auth or validation tests
Review the affected trust boundary
Document residual risk

commonMisroutes.query: upgrade vulnerable dependency and prove compatibility
commonMisroutes.reason: Ongoing dependency advisory checks belong to the dependency watch pattern.

reliability.summary: Reliable when the security risk is specific and has a regression or review check.
bestWhen: The affected boundary is known.
The proof can run locally.
failsWhen: The work requires secret rotation or production changes.
The finding is too broad to verify.
reliability.requiredCheck: Run the relevant security, auth, validation, or regression check.

userGuide.plainLanguage: Use this when the work is reducing a named security risk.
userGuide.useWhen: Pick this for auth, validation, secret, or permission fixes with proof.
userGuide.starterRequest: Sanitize unsafe user input before saving it.
userGuide.firstStep: Identify the trust boundary and the failing or missing guard.
userGuide.proofTip: Run the relevant validation, auth, or regression check and name residual risk.
userGuide.notFor: Not for broad security audits or secret rotation without approval.

loops.title: Release readiness
loops.summary: Prepare a package, app, or feature for a public release with evidence.
bestFor: Final package, app, or feature release checks.
Dry-run packaging, install smoke, changelog, and release notes.
avoidWhen: Core work is still incomplete.
Publishing requires missing OTP or approval.
The request is only dependency watch scheduling.
loops.defaultObjective: Prepare the release and prove it is ready to ship.
loops.defaultCheck: Run package checks, dry-run publish or build, and a fresh install or production smoke.
stopConditions: Stop when release checks pass and required metadata is updated.
Stop if publish, deploy, or credentials need user approval.
Stop if public verification fails after the release artifact changes.
questions: What version or artifact is being released?
Which public or fresh-install check proves it shipped correctly?
loops.body: DISCOVER release surface, PLAN checks, EXECUTE metadata and package prep, VERIFY dry-run and fresh install, then STOP before approval-sensitive publish unless approved.
goodExamples: publish npm package after final release checks
publish npm package
prepare 0.3.1 release
verify latest npx install works
fix stale installed skill copies
badExamples: core feature is still incomplete
dependency upgrade compatibility only
publishing needs missing OTP approval
exampleChecks: Run npm run check
Run npm pack --dry-run or build dry-run
Verify from a fresh install or public URL

commonMisroutes.query: check CI every 5 minutes until the branch is green
commonMisroutes.reason: Polling CI over time is a time-based watch, not release prep.

reliability.summary: Reliable when release work has a known artifact, version impact, and fresh install or public smoke.
bestWhen: The artifact is ready for final checks.
A release verifier exists.
failsWhen: The release needs OTP, deploy approval, or external account access.
Feature work is not finished.
reliability.requiredCheck: Run package checks, dry-run publish or build, and a fresh install or production smoke.

userGuide.plainLanguage: Use this when the code is ready and you need proof before shipping.
userGuide.useWhen: Pick this for package release, app deploy readiness, changelog, and fresh install checks.
userGuide.starterRequest: Publish npm package after final release checks.
userGuide.firstStep: List the release surface: version, docs, package contents, and verifier.
userGuide.proofTip: Show check output, dry-run package contents, and fresh install or public smoke proof.
userGuide.notFor: Not for unfinished feature work or background CI polling.

loops.title: PR review watch
loops.summary: Poll a pull request on an interval and handle new review comments until approval or a blocker appears; requires a host scheduler/connector.
bestFor: A PR needs repeated checks for new comments or status changes.
The host can poll GitHub or another review system.
avoidWhen: There is only a local diff to review once.
No connector or schedule can read the PR.
Comments require product approval.
loops.defaultObjective: Watch the PR for new review comments and resolve actionable ones with proof.
loops.defaultCheck: Every interval, fetch new review comments and CI status; stop when approved, merged, blocked, or the interval budget ends.
stopConditions: Stop when the PR is approved or merged.
Stop when no new comments appear for the configured interval budget.
Stop if a comment needs a product decision or unsafe action.
questions: Which PR should be watched?
What interval and max passes should the host use?
Which checks must stay green?
loops.body: SCHEDULE polling in the host, OBSERVE new PR comments and checks, ACT on actionable comments, VERIFY, DECIDE whether to continue, then REPEAT until stop.
goodExamples: watch my PR every 10 minutes and address new review comments
poll this pull request until approval or a blocker appears
check for new review comments every 15 minutes
badExamples: review this diff for risky changes before merge
fix failing CI typecheck job
explain how checkout auth flow works without editing code
exampleChecks: Fetch PR comments and CI status each pass
Run the affected verifier after edits
Record which comments were resolved or blocked

commonMisroutes.query: review this diff for risky changes before merge
commonMisroutes.reason: A one-shot local diff review should not use a scheduled watch.

reliability.summary: Useful as a host pattern, but Loop It does not provide the scheduler or PR connector by itself.
bestWhen: The host has GitHub access.
The PR can be polled safely.
failsWhen: No connector is available.
The review comments need human product decisions.
reliability.requiredCheck: Host must confirm each poll fetched PR state and any changed code passed the relevant verifier.

userGuide.plainLanguage: Use this when a PR needs repeated review-comment checks.
userGuide.useWhen: Pick this only if your host can poll the PR over time.
userGuide.starterRequest: Watch my PR every 10 minutes and address new review comments.
userGuide.firstStep: Confirm the PR URL, interval, max passes, and checks to keep green.
userGuide.proofTip: Each pass should list new comments, actions taken, and verifier results.
userGuide.notFor: Not for a single local diff review.

loops.title: CI health watch
loops.summary: Poll CI on a schedule until the branch is green, stable-failing, or blocked; requires a host scheduler/connector.
bestFor: CI status is still running or intermittently changing.
The next action depends on future check results.
avoidWhen: A specific failing command is already known.
The host cannot read CI status.
The branch needs immediate local repair.
loops.defaultObjective: Watch CI until the branch is green or the failure is stable enough to repair.
loops.defaultCheck: Every interval, fetch CI status and logs; stop when green, stable-failing, or blocked.
stopConditions: Stop when all required checks are green.
Stop when the same failure repeats twice with stable logs.
Stop when CI cannot be read by the host.
questions: Which branch or PR should be watched?
What interval and max passes are safe?
Which CI checks are required?
loops.body: SCHEDULE polling, OBSERVE CI status and logs, DECIDE whether to wait or hand off to failing-ci-repair, and REPEAT until green or stable-failing.
goodExamples: check CI every 5 minutes until the branch is green
watch status checks until the deployment workflow finishes
poll CI until the same failure repeats twice
badExamples: fix failing CI typecheck job
publish npm package after final release checks
review this diff for risky changes before merge
exampleChecks: Fetch status checks each pass
Capture logs when a failure appears
Hand off to failing-ci-repair once the failure is stable

commonMisroutes.query: fix failing CI typecheck job
commonMisroutes.reason: Known failures should be repaired directly instead of watched.

reliability.summary: Useful for waiting on remote CI, but depends on host access to CI status.
bestWhen: CI is pending or flaky.
The host can fetch logs and statuses.
failsWhen: The failing command is already known.
CI access is unavailable.
reliability.requiredCheck: Each interval must record CI status, log URL or excerpt, and next action.

userGuide.plainLanguage: Use this when CI is still moving and needs polling.
userGuide.useWhen: Pick this when the job is to wait, observe, and react to CI status.
userGuide.starterRequest: Check CI every 5 minutes until the branch is green.
userGuide.firstStep: Confirm the branch or PR and which checks matter.
userGuide.proofTip: The log should show every poll result and why the loop stopped.
userGuide.notFor: Not for a known local failing command.

loops.title: Daily dependency watch
loops.summary: Run a scheduled dependency and advisory check, then open or recommend one bounded upgrade loop when needed.
bestFor: Dependency updates or advisories need regular review.
The host can run scheduled package checks.
avoidWhen: A specific vulnerable code path needs a fix now.
The repo has no lockfile or package manager.
Bulk upgrades would be unsafe.
loops.defaultObjective: Check dependencies on schedule and route actionable upgrades to a bounded follow-up.
loops.defaultCheck: Run the package-manager audit/outdated command and the project compatibility check.
stopConditions: Stop when no actionable updates are found.
Stop after creating or recommending one bounded upgrade follow-up.
Stop if the update requires approval or major-version product risk.
questions: Which package manager and schedule should be used?
Which severity threshold should trigger a follow-up?
loops.body: SCHEDULE the dependency check, OBSERVE audit/outdated output, DECIDE if one upgrade follow-up is warranted, and STOP with proof or a routed loop.
goodExamples: run a daily dependency update check and report advisories
schedule npm audit and route moderate advisories
check outdated dependencies every morning
badExamples: sanitize unsafe user input before saving it
upgrade React dependency and fix compatibility issues now
publish npm package after final release checks
exampleChecks: Run npm audit or equivalent
Run npm outdated or equivalent when safe
Run the project compatibility check before recommending changes

commonMisroutes.query: sanitize unsafe user input before saving it
commonMisroutes.reason: Application security fixes should use the security hardening loop.

reliability.summary: Reliable as a scheduled detection pattern; actual upgrades should be handed to a bounded repair loop.
bestWhen: The repo has a lockfile.
A package-manager audit command exists.
failsWhen: The host cannot schedule commands.
Upgrades require broad manual QA.
reliability.requiredCheck: The scheduled run must record audit/outdated output and the compatibility verifier result.

userGuide.plainLanguage: Use this for recurring dependency checks.
userGuide.useWhen: Pick this when advisories should be checked on a schedule, not fixed ad hoc.
userGuide.starterRequest: Run a daily dependency update check and report advisories.
userGuide.firstStep: Confirm package manager, schedule, severity threshold, and compatibility check.
userGuide.proofTip: Record audit output and whether a follow-up upgrade loop was recommended.
userGuide.notFor: Not for immediate app security bugs or broad dependency rewrites.

loops.title: Docs freshness watch
loops.summary: Periodically check setup docs, commands, and examples for drift against the current package or product state.
bestFor: Docs drift recurs after releases.
Install commands or examples should be checked on a cadence.
avoidWhen: There is one known typo to fix now.
The docs need a full rewrite.
The host cannot run scheduled checks.
loops.defaultObjective: Check docs freshness and fix or route one stale instruction with proof.
loops.defaultCheck: Run documented setup commands or the closest docs smoke from a clean checkout.
stopConditions: Stop when docs match current commands and versions.
Stop after fixing or routing one stale instruction.
Stop if validating docs requires unavailable external services.
questions: Which docs are source-of-truth?
Which commands prove freshness?
How often should the host run this?
loops.body: SCHEDULE docs checks, OBSERVE command/version drift, ACT on one stale item or route a follow-up, VERIFY, and STOP.
goodExamples: check docs once a week for stale install commands
update stale README installation docs after npx command changed
run weekly docs freshness check for setup examples
badExamples: make one small copy edit and verify lint still passes
publish npm package after final release checks
watch my PR every 10 minutes and address new review comments
exampleChecks: Run documented commands from a clean directory when possible
Compare documented version or command against package metadata
Record any stale docs and the proof after fixing

commonMisroutes.query: make one small copy edit
commonMisroutes.reason: A one-off copy edit should stay turn-based, not scheduled.

reliability.summary: Good for recurring drift detection; depends on the host being able to run scheduled checks.
bestWhen: Docs contain commands or versions that can drift.
A clean setup check exists.
failsWhen: Docs require subjective rewriting.
The host cannot schedule the watch.
reliability.requiredCheck: Each run must record the docs checked, command output, and any changed files.

userGuide.plainLanguage: Use this to keep docs from drifting after releases.
userGuide.useWhen: Pick this for recurring install-command or example freshness checks.
userGuide.starterRequest: Check docs once a week for stale install commands.
userGuide.firstStep: Identify docs that drift and the command that proves them.
userGuide.proofTip: Show the documented command running or explain the exact blocker.
userGuide.notFor: Not for one tiny typo or subjective documentation rewrites.

loops.title: Production smoke watch
loops.summary: Run a scheduled public smoke check and report or route failures without silently changing production.
bestFor: A public URL or shipped flow needs periodic health checks.
Failures should route to a bounded repair loop.
avoidWhen: The check writes production data.
No public or safe smoke path exists.
A known failing deployment needs immediate repair.
loops.defaultObjective: Run the public smoke on schedule and route failures to the right repair loop.
loops.defaultCheck: Run the safe production smoke command or browser check against the public URL.
stopConditions: Stop when the smoke passes.
Stop after routing one failure with evidence.
Stop if the smoke would write production data or need credentials.
questions: Which public URL or smoke command is safe?
What schedule should the host use?
Who should receive failures?
loops.body: SCHEDULE a safe smoke, OBSERVE the public result, DECIDE pass/fail, ROUTE failures with evidence, and STOP without production writes.
goodExamples: run production smoke every morning and report failures
check the public Loop It page daily and route failures
watch the deployed app home page with a safe smoke
badExamples: fix failed Vercel preview deployment build from deploy logs
publish npm package after final release checks
sanitize unsafe user input before saving it
exampleChecks: Run the safe public smoke
Capture status code, screenshot, or command output
Route failures to a repair loop without changing production

commonMisroutes.query: publish npm package after final release checks
commonMisroutes.reason: Final release prep is goal-based, not a recurring production watch.

reliability.summary: Useful for scheduled smoke checks, but it must remain read-only unless the user approves otherwise.
bestWhen: A safe public smoke exists.
The host can schedule and report failures.
failsWhen: The smoke writes data or needs credentials.
Failures require manual incident decisions.
reliability.requiredCheck: Every run must record the public URL or command, result, and routed next action.

userGuide.plainLanguage: Use this to check a shipped app or page on a schedule.
userGuide.useWhen: Pick this for read-only public smoke checks.
userGuide.starterRequest: Run production smoke every morning and report failures.
userGuide.firstStep: Confirm the URL or command is safe and read-only.
userGuide.proofTip: Record status, screenshot or output, and the routed follow-up if it fails.
userGuide.notFor: Not for deploys, production writes, or immediate known failures.

loops.title: Incoming bug triage routine
loops.summary: React to incoming bug reports, classify them, and route one actionable item to a bounded fix loop; requires an issue/support connector.
bestFor: New bug reports arrive in an issue tracker or support queue.
The routine should classify, deduplicate, and route work.
avoidWhen: There is one known bug to fix now.
No queue connector is available.
The report contains sensitive customer data that cannot be processed.
loops.defaultObjective: Triage incoming bug reports and route the highest-confidence actionable item.
loops.defaultCheck: Read new queue items, classify severity and reproducibility, and create or recommend one bounded follow-up loop.
stopConditions: Stop after routing one actionable item or reporting no actionable bugs.
Stop if triage needs private data access or product decisions.
Stop if duplicate detection is inconclusive.
questions: Which queue or tracker should be watched?
What severity rules should be used?
Where should routed work be recorded?
loops.body: ON EVENT, OBSERVE new bug reports, CLASSIFY and deduplicate, DECIDE the next bounded loop, ROUTE it with evidence, and STOP.
goodExamples: triage incoming bug reports from the issue queue and route them
classify new support bugs and create one verified fix follow-up
process new bug reports and pick the highest severity actionable issue
badExamples: fix bug where checkout redirects to the wrong page
review this diff for risky changes before merge
run production smoke every morning and report failures
exampleChecks: Record item IDs reviewed
Classify severity and reproducibility
Route one bug to ticket-to-verified-fix or report no action

commonMisroutes.query: fix bug where checkout redirects to the wrong page
commonMisroutes.reason: A known bug should be fixed directly.

reliability.summary: Useful as an event-driven triage pattern, but requires a real queue connector and routing rules.
bestWhen: Bug reports arrive in a structured queue.
Routing rules are clear.
failsWhen: No connector is available.
Reports require human product judgment or sensitive-data handling.
reliability.requiredCheck: The routine must record reviewed item IDs, classification, and routed loop or no-action reason.

userGuide.plainLanguage: Use this when new bug reports should be sorted automatically.
userGuide.useWhen: Pick this only when a host can read the issue or support queue.
userGuide.starterRequest: Triage incoming bug reports from the issue queue and route them.
userGuide.firstStep: Confirm queue access, severity rules, and routing destination.
userGuide.proofTip: Show which items were reviewed and why one was routed or skipped.
userGuide.notFor: Not for fixing one already-known bug.

loops.title: Dependency upgrade queue routine
loops.summary: Process dependency update items as they appear and route safe upgrades into bounded compatibility loops.
bestFor: Dependency PRs or update tickets arrive continuously.
The routine should choose one safe upgrade at a time.
avoidWhen: There is only a scheduled audit with no queue.
The upgrade needs broad migration work.
No compatibility check is available.
loops.defaultObjective: Process the dependency upgrade queue and route one safe update with compatibility proof.
loops.defaultCheck: Inspect new dependency items and run or require the project compatibility check before routing.
stopConditions: Stop after routing or closing one dependency item.
Stop if the update is major-risk or lacks a verifier.
Stop if credentials or external approval are needed.
questions: Which dependency queue should be watched?
What compatibility check proves an upgrade?
Which update classes are allowed automatically?
loops.body: ON EVENT, OBSERVE dependency items, FILTER by risk, ACT on one safe item or route it, VERIFY compatibility, and STOP.
goodExamples: proactively process dependency upgrade tickets as they arrive
handle new Dependabot PRs one at a time with compatibility proof
triage Renovate queue and route safe patch upgrades
badExamples: run a daily dependency update check and report advisories
upgrade React dependency and fix compatibility issues now
sanitize unsafe user input before saving it
exampleChecks: Record dependency item reviewed
Run or name compatibility verifier
Route risky upgrades instead of silently changing them

commonMisroutes.query: run a daily dependency update check
commonMisroutes.reason: Scheduled audits use the time-based dependency watch.

reliability.summary: Useful for a live update queue, but not a substitute for compatibility proof.
bestWhen: The queue is structured.
Patch/minor policy and checks are defined.
failsWhen: The queue has only risky major upgrades.
No verifier exists.
reliability.requiredCheck: Each processed item must include package, version, risk class, and compatibility check result.

userGuide.plainLanguage: Use this when dependency update tickets arrive over time.
userGuide.useWhen: Pick this for Dependabot, Renovate, or upgrade queues.
userGuide.starterRequest: Proactively process dependency upgrade tickets as they arrive.
userGuide.firstStep: Confirm queue source, allowed update classes, and verifier.
userGuide.proofTip: Log the item, risk decision, and compatibility proof.
userGuide.notFor: Not for one manual upgrade or scheduled audit-only checks.

loops.title: Review comment resolver routine
loops.summary: React to new review comments, apply safe fixes, verify, and request another review; requires PR connector access.
bestFor: New review comments arrive after the author has moved on.
The host can read comments and update the PR.
avoidWhen: The user only wants a one-shot diff review.
Comments require product judgment.
No PR connector is available.
loops.defaultObjective: Resolve new actionable PR review comments and request re-review with proof.
loops.defaultCheck: Fetch new comments, apply safe fixes, run the affected verifier, and record resolved comment IDs.
stopConditions: Stop after resolving the current batch or reporting blockers.
Stop if a comment needs product decision or unsafe action.
Stop if verifier fails repeatedly.
questions: Which PR should this routine watch?
What checks must run before requesting re-review?
Which comments require human approval?
loops.body: ON REVIEW EVENT, OBSERVE comments, PLAN safe fixes, ACT on actionable items, VERIFY, REQUEST re-review when allowed, and STOP.
goodExamples: automatically resolve new PR review comments and request review
resolve new review comments on this PR with proof
handle reviewer feedback as it arrives and rerun checks
badExamples: review this diff for risky changes before merge
watch my PR every 10 minutes and address new review comments
fix failing CI typecheck job
exampleChecks: Record comment IDs handled
Run the relevant verifier
Request re-review only after checks pass and approvals allow it

commonMisroutes.query: watch my PR every 10 minutes
commonMisroutes.reason: Interval polling belongs to the time-based PR watch.

reliability.summary: Good for event-driven review queues when connector permissions and verifier policy are clear.
bestWhen: Review comments are actionable and code-local.
The host can update the PR.
failsWhen: Comments need product calls.
The host cannot request review or run checks.
reliability.requiredCheck: The routine must record resolved comment IDs, verifier output, and re-review action or blocker.

userGuide.plainLanguage: Use this when review comments should be handled as they arrive.
userGuide.useWhen: Pick this for event-triggered PR feedback resolution.
userGuide.starterRequest: Automatically resolve new PR review comments and request review.
userGuide.firstStep: Confirm PR access, comment scope, and verifier required before re-review.
userGuide.proofTip: Record comment IDs, changed files, verifier output, and review request status.
userGuide.notFor: Not for one-time diff review or interval polling.

loops.title: Customer feedback action routine
loops.summary: Classify incoming customer feedback and route one actionable product or bug follow-up without sending external replies by default.
bestFor: Feedback arrives in a queue and needs routing.
The routine should separate bug, feature, docs, and no-action items.
avoidWhen: The user wants to send customer messages.
Feedback contains sensitive data without handling rules.
A single known bug should be fixed directly.
loops.defaultObjective: Turn customer feedback messages into routed fixes, tickets, or no-action decisions.
loops.defaultCheck: Classify new feedback items, record IDs and rationale, and route one bounded follow-up when actionable.
stopConditions: Stop after classifying the current batch or routing one action.
Stop before sending external messages without approval.
Stop if feedback requires product prioritization.
questions: Which feedback source should be read?
What classification rules should be used?
Where should routed work go?
loops.body: ON EVENT, OBSERVE feedback, CLASSIFY intent and risk, DECIDE route or no-action, CREATE one bounded follow-up when safe, and STOP.
goodExamples: turn customer feedback messages into fixes or tickets automatically
classify new feedback and route one actionable bug
process support feedback without sending replies
badExamples: fix bug where checkout redirects to the wrong page
send a customer email update
make one small copy edit and verify lint still passes
exampleChecks: Record feedback IDs reviewed
Classify each item
Route one follow-up or state no-action reason

commonMisroutes.query: send a customer email update
commonMisroutes.reason: External messages require explicit approval and are not part of this default routine.

reliability.summary: Useful for routing feedback, but deliberately avoids external replies and product-priority decisions.
bestWhen: Feedback is available in a structured source.
Routing rules are clear.
failsWhen: Feedback requires customer communication.
Sensitive data handling rules are missing.
reliability.requiredCheck: The routine must record feedback IDs, classifications, and routed follow-up or blocker.

userGuide.plainLanguage: Use this to sort incoming customer feedback into action.
userGuide.useWhen: Pick this when a host can read feedback and routing rules are known.
userGuide.starterRequest: Turn customer feedback messages into fixes or tickets automatically.
userGuide.firstStep: Confirm source, classification labels, and routing destination.
userGuide.proofTip: Record item IDs, classifications, and one routed next action.
userGuide.notFor: Not for sending customer messages or deciding product roadmap priority.

loops.title: Weekly code health routine
loops.summary: Periodically find one small code-health improvement, run the verifier, and stop before broad refactors.
bestFor: A repo benefits from small recurring cleanup with proof.
The host can run on a schedule and open a bounded follow-up.
avoidWhen: The repo has urgent failing checks.
The cleanup scope is broad or subjective.
No verifier can guard behavior.
loops.defaultObjective: Find and complete one small code-health improvement with proof.
loops.defaultCheck: Run the project check plus any narrow verifier for the touched area.
stopConditions: Stop after one small cleanup and passing verifier.
Stop if the improvement becomes a broad refactor.
Stop if no safe verifier exists.
questions: Which repo or area should the routine inspect?
Which checks must pass?
What changes are out of scope?
loops.body: ON SCHEDULE, DISCOVER one low-risk code-health issue, ACT with a small patch, VERIFY, REPORT proof, and STOP.
goodExamples: run a weekly code health routine and open small cleanup PRs
proactively find one low-risk cleanup each week
weekly maintenance routine with npm run check proof
badExamples: refactor checkout module without changing behavior
fix failing CI typecheck job
inspect this repo and run the right loop
exampleChecks: Run the project verifier
Show the one cleanup chosen and why it is low risk
Stop after one bounded change

commonMisroutes.query: fix failing CI typecheck job
commonMisroutes.reason: Urgent failing checks should be repaired directly.

reliability.summary: Useful for small recurring maintenance, but dangerous if allowed to become broad refactoring.
bestWhen: Cleanup scope is narrow.
The verifier is cheap and reliable.
failsWhen: The repo has no safe verifier.
The routine tries to batch unrelated refactors.
reliability.requiredCheck: Every run must record the selected cleanup, changed files, and verifier output.

userGuide.plainLanguage: Use this for small recurring maintenance, one improvement at a time.
userGuide.useWhen: Pick this when a scheduled routine should keep code health tidy.
userGuide.starterRequest: Run a weekly code health routine and open small cleanup PRs.
userGuide.firstStep: Confirm allowed scope, schedule, and project verifier.
userGuide.proofTip: Stop after one low-risk change and show verifier output.
userGuide.notFor: Not for urgent failures, broad refactors, or subjective cleanup.
